items.q: Does repeating an idea over and over count as developing it?
items.correct: No - development means transforming the idea, for example by sequence, inversion, augmentation or fragmentation.
items.misconception: Development just means repeating your main idea many times.
items.random_wrong: Development means deleting the idea and writing a new one each phrase.
items.why: Exact repetition keeps material static; examiners look for recognisable ideas treated in varied ways, which is what creates a sense of growth in a composition.

items.q: Will using as many instruments as possible make a composition better?
items.correct: No - effective composing controls texture, using contrast between thick and thin scoring; every part should earn its place.
items.misconception: A bigger ensemble automatically means a more impressive composition.
items.random_wrong: Compositions are only allowed to use four instruments.
items.why: Texture is an expressive resource: constant full scoring becomes grey and unbalanced, while deliberate contrast of layers gives the music shape and highlights climaxes.

items.q: What is the difference between inversion and retrograde?
items.correct: Inversion turns the melodic intervals upside down; retrograde plays the idea backwards in time.
items.misconception: Inversion means playing the melody backwards.
items.random_wrong: Inversion means doubling the melody in octaves.
items.why: Both are mirror techniques, so students conflate them: inversion mirrors pitch direction around an axis, whereas retrograde mirrors the order of the notes.

items.q: Repeating a melodic idea immediately at a higher or lower pitch is called what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sequence. “Inversion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Writing parts that suit the range and technique of each instrument is described as what?
items.why: It's Idiomatic writing. “Strophic writing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A texture of one melody supported by chordal accompaniment is called what?
items.why: It's Homophonic. “Monophonic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Augmentation develops a theme by doing what?
items.why: It's Lengthening its note values proportionally. “Playing it backwards” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a rondo structured ABACA, how many times is the main A theme heard?
items.why: The real figure is 3 times. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
